skill.name: claude-chrome
skill.description: Use Claude with Chrome browser automation to navigate pages, test web apps, fill forms, inspect pages, and run guided workflows in authenticated sessions.
skill.summary: Control Chrome with Claude for web testing, form work, page inspection, and authenticated browser workflows.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static command detections are primarily false positives from Markdown examples, not executable skill code. The skill still carries medium risk because it documents authenticated Chrome automation with screenshots, file upload, JavaScript evaluation, network inspection, and an unpinned MCP install command.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Browser Automation Can Access Sensitive Accounts
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill instructs Claude to use a real logged-in Chrome session and work with Gmail, Google Docs, Notion, CRMs, Google Analytics, and scheduled tasks. This is legitimate browser automation, but it can expose private account data if permissions and confirmations are not tightly controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes use of existing browser logins and examples involving private workspaces, email, documents, and analytics. The risk is capability exposure, not evidence of malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Browser Tools Include Screenshots, Uploads, Script Evaluation, and Network Inspection
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The documented MCP tools include file upload, page screenshot, DOM snapshot, JavaScript execution, console log access, and network request inspection. These are expected automation features, but they can capture sensitive content or modify page state in authenticated sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed tools directly match the sensitive capabilities in the static findings. Their presence is documented and intentional, with legitimate use possible.

medium_findings.title: Unpinned MCP Package Installation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill recommends installing chrome-devtools-mcp with @latest through npx and also shows @latest in MCP configuration. Using a moving package version increases supply-chain review risk because future package contents can change without a skill update.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear documentation rather than hidden execution, but @latest is visible and creates a real supply-chain control gap for users who follow the instructions.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Triggered External Command Alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Most Ruby or shell backtick detections point to Markdown code fences, inline command names, or quoted user prompts. The skill contains documentation only and does not execute those commands by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious locations are rendered as examples and prompts in SKILL.md. No executable Ruby, shell wrapper, or script file was found in the reviewed skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Localhost URL Is a Local Debug Example
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URL is http://localhost:9222 in a command for connecting Chrome DevTools MCP to a local browser. No evidence found that data is sent to an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is loopback-only and appears under configuration examples. It does not identify a remote endpoint or exfiltration path.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Unsupported by Context
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts align with ordinary Chrome-related text, and reconnaissance alerts align with requirements or permission notes. No evidence found of cryptography, system probing, or network reconnaissance logic in the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose about Chrome features, paid plans, generated shortcut metadata, and site permissions. They do not contain implementation logic for crypto or reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Logged-In Browser Session Control
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs use of existing Chrome login state, which can expose private site data during automation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly states that Claude uses the real browser session and existing logins.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Browser Capture and File Transfer Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented tool list includes upload_file, take_screenshot, and take_snapshot, which can move or capture sensitive page and file content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool names and descriptions directly show upload and capture capability. The risk depends on user permissions and site context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Script Evaluation and Network Request Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented DevTools tools include JavaScript evaluation and network request inspection inside browser pages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capability is explicitly listed in the browser debugging and network tool sections. It is expected for DevTools, but security-sensitive.

content.value_statement: Manual browser tasks waste time and are hard to repeat consistently. This skill helps Claude control Chrome for testing, form entry, research, and authenticated workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to start Claude Code with Chrome browser control enabled.
Documents Chrome DevTools MCP setup through Claude MCP configuration.
Lists browser automation actions such as navigation, clicking, typing, scrolling, tabs, and resizing.
Describes page inspection features including DOM snapshots, console logs, screenshots, and network requests.
Provides prompt examples for form testing, data extraction, email drafts, and content updates.
Covers workflow shortcuts, scheduled tasks, and multi-tab workflows.
limitations: Requires Google Chrome, the Claude Chrome extension, Claude Code, and a paid Claude plan.
Does not support other Chromium browsers, WSL, mobile devices, or extension headless mode.
Authenticated automation depends on site permissions and user confirmation for high-risk actions.
The skill is documentation only and does not bundle executable automation code.

use_cases.title: Test Local Web Applications
use_cases.description: Run browser flows against a local development site, inspect form behavior, and check console errors.

use_cases.title: Maintain Business Web Tools
use_cases.description: Fill repeated CRM or vendor forms from structured source data while keeping review before submission.

use_cases.title: Collect Authenticated Page Summaries
use_cases.description: Open permitted dashboards or documents and summarize information that requires an existing login.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open Chrome to the target page, summarize what is visible, and tell me if any login or permission step blocks progress.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Open my local app, test the main form with valid and invalid inputs, check visible errors, and capture a screenshot of any issue.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Page Data
prompt_templates.scenario: Structured research from an authenticated page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the approved dashboard, collect the visible weekly metrics, summarize them in a table, and do not change any settings.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Reviewed Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Chrome to complete the vendor intake workflow from my source notes, pause before final submission, and report every field you changed.

output_examples.input: Test the login form on localhost and check the console.
output_examples.output: The login form loaded, invalid credentials showed an error message, and the console had one validation warning. No submission was completed.

output_examples.input: Summarize this week from my analytics dashboard.
output: Traffic increased compared with last week.
The highest source was organic search.
One chart could not be read until the page finished loading.

output_examples.input: Draft replies for unread engineering emails without sending them.
output_examples.output: Draft replies were prepared for three messages. No messages were sent, and each draft is ready for your review in Gmail.

best_practices: Grant site permissions only for pages needed by the current workflow.
Ask Claude to pause before publishing, purchasing, sharing data, or submitting forms.
Use specific prompts that define the page, expected action, and verification step.
anti_patterns: Do not give broad access to sensitive accounts without a clear task boundary.
Do not ask for bulk extraction from private pages unless you are authorized to export it.
Do not rely on scheduled tasks for actions that require human judgment or approval.

faq.question: Does this skill run browser automation code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill that explains how to use Claude Code, the Chrome extension, and Chrome DevTools MCP.

faq.question: Can it use websites where I am already logged in?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow uses your Chrome session, so site permissions and careful review are important.

faq.question: Can it work with Chrome DevTools MCP?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes setup commands and examples for chrome-devtools-mcp.

faq.question: Can it upload files or take screenshots?
faq.answer: The documented tool set includes file upload and screenshot capabilities when those tools are available.

faq.question: Is it safe for financial or highly regulated websites?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill notes that some site categories are blocked and high-risk actions require confirmation.

faq.question: What should I review before using scheduled tasks?
faq.answer: Review the site permissions, target pages, task frequency, and any action that could publish, purchase, or share data.
